section_text: They receive no rewards, so their direct behavioral response to being rewarded is unknown. There is no evidence of reciprocal reward or of forming implicit cooperative “clubs” through mutual bonuses. Given their general avoidance of both punishment and reward despite seeing others spend in these ways, it is uncertain whether they would reciprocate if rewarded; any assumption about gratitude signaling or alliance-building from them would be speculative. Mark this dimension as unknown.

This player receives no rewards at any point, so their reaction to identifiable rewarders cannot be inferred. Their tendencies for reciprocating rewards, forming alliances, or increasing cooperation in response to being rewarded are unknown.

This player is repeatedly rewarded by others, including episodes of very large positive transfers, yet they never respond with reciprocal rewards. They also do not visibly alter their own contribution pattern in a way that clearly favors those who rewarded them; their contributions remain in a similar mid-range band, without identifiable spikes that coincide with receiving help. This suggests they do not use the reward channel to signal gratitude, repay benefactors, or cement alliances. Their behavior is consistent with either viewing rewards as windfalls unconnected to future obligation, or with concentrating on their own payoff irrespective of social gestures. Their stance toward identifiable rewarders is behaviorally neutral: they neither repay them via reward nor distinguish them via notable changes in cooperative effort.

They receive rewards in several rounds from different peers, including when they are contributing fully and when they are free-riding. Despite this, they never respond with reciprocal rewards, and they do not visibly adjust their contribution behavior in a way that favors those who rewarded them. When rewarded after cooperative behavior, they do not become consistently more cooperative; when rewarded while free-riding, they do not show signs of guilt-driven correction or loyalty formation. Because rewarders’ identities are visible, their lack of targeted reciprocation suggests that they do not use reward as a basis for alliance formation or reputational exchange. Their stance toward rewarders appears to be “quietly exploitative” or at least strongly self-regarding: they accept the benefits but do not treat them as obligations or signals requiring a response. Their social reciprocity, at least via the available monetary channels, is effectively absent.

They receive rewards multiple times but never reciprocate with rewards or with explicit verbal thanks. They also do not adjust their reward behavior from “never reward” to “sometimes reward” in response to being rewarded, suggesting a low propensity for direct reciprocity via the reward channel. Any reciprocity they may express appears limited to their own contribution decisions (whether they reward cooperative rewarders by continued cooperation is unclear and low confidence). They do not engage in alliance-building or tit-for-tat reward exchanges, and they do not appear to use identifiable rewarders as special partners. Their emotional reaction (gratitude, indifference, annoyance) is unknown; behaviorally they remain stoic and unmoved in their use of the reward tool.

They receive reward in every round from identifiable others but never reciprocate with reward, either to specific rewarders or to any contributor. They also do not respond in chat, so any gratitude, alliance-building, or emotional response is unobserved. Their contribution pattern does not change following rewards—they remain at full contribution, which they already chose before any reward was experienced and maintain regardless of reward variation. Behaviorally, they treat rewards as a passive income stream rather than something to be reciprocated or strategically leveraged. This implies low behavioral reciprocity in the reward dimension: they are a first-order cooperator but not a reciprocal rewarder. Whether they feel thankful internally is unknown; there is no observable signaling of appreciation or alliance formation.

When others reward them, they do not adjust their behavior in any visible way: they were already fully cooperative, and they remain so. They also do not reciprocate by rewarding anyone in return, including likely rewarders, despite being able to identify them. This indicates low propensity for direct reciprocity through the reward mechanism. They neither build alliances via mutual rewarding nor shift into any special favoritism; their pattern stays rule-based and impersonal. The rewards they receive do not induce them to change their contribution pattern, which was already maximally cooperative, and do not induce them to start using reward as a tool. This suggests that they treat rewards as incidental rather than as strategic signals to which they must respond, and that their cooperative rule is internally motivated rather than contingent on receiving positive reinforcement.

The player receives rewards in several instances but never reciprocates with rewards of their own, either toward specific individuals or to the group of high contributors more generally. Their contribution level remains unchanged and already at the maximum, so there is no observable “upward shift” in response to being rewarded. They also do not increase their willingness to use the reward or punishment mechanisms after being positively targeted. This pattern suggests that they do not treat rewards as a cue for forming special alliances or for engaging in mutual support through side payments. Instead, they continue to follow their existing cooperative rule while ignoring the opportunity to respond in kind. Whether they feel gratitude internally is unknown, but behaviorally they do not translate being rewarded into reciprocal transfers or strategic coalition-building.

When rewarded, this player does not engage in explicit reciprocal rewarding or targeted positive responses. Their behavior does not change in the direction of using the reward mechanism themselves, and they do not appear to selectively favor or punish particular individuals based on having been rewarded. However, their contributions remain generally high in a cooperative environment, which may indicate that reward contributes to maintaining their willingness to cooperate, even if they do not mirror the act directly. They show no sign of building dyadic alliances through reward exchange or of shifting to preferential treatment of specific rewarders; their responses are more global than relational, focusing on overall group norms rather than on individual ties. Overall, they are appreciative in behavior only at the group level (sustaining cooperation), not in targeted reciprocity toward identifiable rewarders.

They do receive rewards from others at several points but do not alter their own contribution strategy or sanctioning style in any observable way in response. Their cooperation remains maximal and stable before and after being rewarded, so there is no visible behavioral shift attributable to gratitude or reciprocity. They also do not appear to engage in direct, targeted reciprocal rewarding back to specific known rewarders; instead, their rewards are distributed to cooperators more generally, regardless of past transfers to them. This suggests that their primary reference point is the cooperation norm itself rather than dyadic reciprocity or alliance building. Their response to being rewarded is best characterized as appreciative but behaviorally unchanged: they continue to follow their pre-existing cooperative and norm-supporting strategy rather than adapting specifically to favor their benefactors.

When rewarded, the player does not shift into a more cooperative or more generous strategy; they soon revert to free-riding and continue to avoid using the reward option themselves. This implies low reciprocity toward rewarders and a weak inclination to form cooperative alliances based on positive treatment. The received reward does not evidently change their approach to contributions or to social transfers. They appear to treat rewards as windfall gains rather than as part of an ongoing relationship or norm of mutual support. As a result, their type can be described as “reward-insensitive” in terms of behavioral updating, with gratitude—if present—remaining purely internal and not expressed through changed actions.

This player is repeatedly rewarded by others, often substantially, yet their pattern of contributions and rewarding does not visibly change in a strategic, tit‑for‑tat manner. They neither single out particular rewarders for special reciprocal treatment nor withhold rewards from non‑rewarders; instead, they continue a broad, group‑level rewarding pattern. This suggests they are not primarily using rewards to build bilateral alliances or engage in direct reciprocity with identifiable partners. Nonetheless, they maintain and even escalate costly reward behavior over time, which is consistent with interpreting incoming rewards as confirmation that their cooperative/rewarding norm is shared or appreciated. Their reaction can be described as generalized reciprocity or group‑level gratitude: rather than targeting specific benefactors, they “pay it forward” to the group. Because we do not see them sharply increasing or decreasing rewards to any individual immediately after being rewarded by that individual, fine‑grained contingent reciprocity is unknown or low. Overall, they respond to being rewarded by staying committed to a high‑cooperation, high‑reward strategy, but not by forming narrow favoritism patterns toward particular rewarders.

The player receives nontrivial amounts of reward from others and responds by sustaining both high cooperation and substantial rewarding of peers. They do not reduce contributions once they see that they are personally being rewarded; instead, they maintain full contribution, consistent with interpreting incoming rewards as confirmation of a shared cooperative norm. Their own reward behavior becomes more generous in environments where they are also being rewarded, consistent with reciprocity or alliance-building: they appear more willing to spend on reward when they observe that others likewise recognize and support cooperation. They seem to treat reward as part of a mutual support system among cooperators rather than as a purely individual gain, and they continue to invest in rewarding even when they could simply pocket the extra benefit. This indicates a reciprocal, coalition-oriented style: respond positively to those who reward and cooperate, reinforce them in turn, and help stabilize a cooperative subgroup within a mixed group. Specific targeting of particular rewarders versus general cooperators is unknown, but the aggregate pattern is consistent with responsive, pro-social reciprocity rather than one-sided altruism.

The player receives rewards from others at several points, including when they contribute very little. They never reciprocate by rewarding those who rewarded them, nor do they noticeably upgrade their contributions to match or thank rewarders in a systematic way. Any occasional increase in contribution after being rewarded is inconsistent and not clearly tied to specific rewarders, making reciprocity, alliance formation, or gratitude signaling at best weak and more likely absent. They thus respond to incoming rewards as a windfall rather than a cue for relational obligations or strategic cooperation.

This player is repeatedly rewarded by others while maintaining high contributions, yet they do not establish clear reciprocal reward ties. They do not reliably respond to incoming rewards by sending rewards back, even when the mechanics allow them to identify and selectively reward contributors. Their contribution level remains high regardless of whether they are being rewarded more or less, suggesting that they do not condition their cooperation on receiving positive reinforcement. The late, large reward outlay does not align in a simple one-to-one way with previously received rewards and is not followed by a new pattern of reciprocity. Thus there is no strong evidence of targeted alliance building or tit-for-tat gratitude toward specific rewarders. Their response to being rewarded is mainly behavioral stability rather than strategic adjustment: they keep cooperating at roughly the same high level and mostly ignore the reward channel. In sum, when rewarders are identifiable, this player does not systematically reciprocate, does not appear to form explicit reward-based alliances, and treats received rewards more as incidental benefits than as obligations or signals to which they must respond.

The player is rewarded at one point but does not change their explicit communication or enforcement behavior in response, and they do not subsequently start rewarding others. Any internal sense of gratitude is unobservable; they offer no public acknowledgment or reciprocal action. Their contribution path after being rewarded returns to high cooperation, but this is also consistent with their earlier cooperative baseline, so whether the reward causally reinforces them is unknown. They do not attempt to form a visible alliance with the rewarder or mimic the rewarder’s enforcement strategy. In effect, they accept rewards silently and continue to make contribution decisions according to their own internal rule rather than visibly engaging in reciprocal social exchange.

They are never recorded as receiving any reward, so their response to being rewarded is unknown. There is no information on whether they would reciprocate rewards or form cooperative alliances with identifiable rewarders.

They are never rewarded, so their reaction style to receiving rewards is unknown. There is no evidence about whether they would reciprocate rewards, form alliances with rewarders, or adjust behavior in response to positive reinforcement.

When rewarded by others, the player does not switch to privileging those specific individuals with exclusive rewards, but they also do not ignore them. They continue to reward many others, including some who reward them and some who do not. There is thus some indirect compatibility with reciprocity—rewarders remain in their favored cooperative set—but not a tight one-to-one reciprocal pattern. Their main driver seems to be others’ cooperative contributions rather than whether they personally received a reward. They do not form clear dyadic alliances; instead, they maintain a broadly inclusive, group-focused rewarding strategy. Gratitude may be present but is not decisive in their allocation choices; prosocial reinforcement appears to dominate personal reciprocity. Overall type (implicit across sections): This player is a “non-punitive altruistic rewarder”: a highly cooperative individual who accepts personal cost to encourage group cooperation, relies almost exclusively on positive reinforcement, largely ignores opportunities for punitive enforcement or retaliation, and maintains cooperation even under end-game and exploitation pressures.

They never receive any rewards, so their response to being rewarded is unknown. There is no evidence about whether they would reciprocate, verbally thank, or form alliances with identifiable rewarders.

They never receive a reward, so their direct response is unknown. Indirectly, they do publicly disclose that they rewarded someone earlier, which may function as a subtle bid for mutual recognition and group bonding rather than a request for reciprocation. There is no evidence of explicit attempts to build bilateral alliances around rewards or to track who deserves future rewards. Any detailed assumptions about reciprocity toward identified rewarders would be speculative and should be treated as unknown.

They receive rewards multiple times and respond by continuing to contribute fully and to participate positively in group chat. They do not explicitly thank specific rewarders, but they acknowledge and reciprocate the cooperative norm at the group level (e.g., endorsing that they are a team, maintaining full contributions, supporting other cooperators in chat). There is no evidence that receiving rewards changes their punishing behavior: they continue to punish defectors aggressively and do not spare previously punished targets because of receiving any reward themselves. They also do not attempt to form explicit bilateral alliances with their rewarders through targeted payback or protection. Overall, they treat rewards as a pleasant but non-strategic signal of group cooperation; they remain committed to their own norm-enforcement and high-contribution strategy regardless of who rewards them.

They occasionally receive rewards from others, but they never comment on them or acknowledge the senders in chat. They do not reciprocate with material rewards, nor do they appear to form explicit alliances or special positive treatment of those who rewarded them. Their contribution behavior remains unchanged—already at maximum—and their punishment pattern is driven by norm-breaking and personal loss rather than by reward relationships. This indicates that while they accept rewards passively, they are not especially reward-responsive in their observable behavior; gratitude, if present, is not expressed or leveraged strategically.

Their own contribution and sanctioning patterns do not map cleanly onto who has rewarded them, but data here are sparse. They receive some rewards, yet they do not develop a clear reciprocal reward loop specifically targeted to those rewarders; they often direct large rewards toward a chronic free-rider instead. This suggests that gratitude-based reciprocity toward rewarders is not their main organizing rule. Their main response to being rewarded appears to be to continue their pre-existing cooperative and generous pattern rather than to condition behavior tightly on who rewarded them. More fine-grained reciprocity norms (such as matching reward levels or forming explicit “alliances”) are unknown or at most weakly expressed.

Unknown. The player is never rewarded, and so there is no direct evidence of how they respond to identifiable rewarders or whether they would reciprocate reward or express gratitude. Their polite and cooperative style suggests they might respond prosocially, but this is speculative and not grounded in observed behavior.

When others reward them, they do not reciprocate with rewards. They never establish a mutual-reward relationship with the players who invest heavily in rewarding. Instead, their responses show up in contribution choices, not in use of the reward mechanism. They do not increase contributions specifically toward those who reward them more, nor do they clearly condition behavior on who supports them. Their pattern suggests they appreciate the extra payoff instrumentally but do not engage in gratitude signaling via rewards. In terms of social strategy, they do not appear to seek alliances or stable coalitions based on the reward network; they remain essentially detached from that layer of interaction.

When they receive rewards, they do not explicitly call out or thank specific rewarders in the chat, nor do they publicly pledge reciprocal reward behavior. Any reciprocity they practice is implicit and cannot be directly inferred, since the transcript does not show who they reward. They also do not complain about under‑rewarding or about not being rewarded enough, even though they are clearly contributing fully and often rewarding others; this points to low entitlement and a lack of transactional framing (“I gave, so I must get back”). Their pro‑reward behavior persists regardless of whether they appear to be heavily or lightly rewarded in a given phase, suggesting that their rewarding is not contingent on personal treatment by others. Overall, their response to being rewarded is behaviorally steady rather than reactive: they remain a full contributor and periodic generous rewarder, with no observable escalation, withdrawal, or targeted alliance‑building in direct response to who rewards them.

They do not explicitly thank or acknowledge specific rewarders, even when they receive substantial rewards. There are no direct “thanks” messages or alliance-building statements aimed at identifiable benefactors. Their one playful comment about the free-riding ape is not directed at any reward relationship. This lack of verbal reciprocity does not mean they ignore rewards behaviorally: given their steady willingness to spend on rewards, it is plausible (though unconfirmed) that they partly reciprocate to those who have rewarded them, but this cannot be established from chat alone. Socially, they respond to being rewarded with continued cooperation and continued use of the reward system, not with explicit gratitude, coalition talk, or attempts to coordinate mutual rewarding. Overall, their response pattern looks like quiet, action-based prosociality rather than overt relational management; they appreciate the cooperative environment enough to keep contributing and rewarding but do not outwardly curate reputational ties.

Their response to being rewarded is understated and pragmatic. They occasionally note in chat that they haven’t been rewarded much and then continue to reward others anyway, which indicates low entitlement and a weak demand for strict reciprocity. There is no clear evidence they systematically direct more rewards back to those who reward them; because identities are visible, they could do so, but their high, broad spending pattern suggests they are not narrowly favoring particular partners. Their behavior implies that rewards function more as a confirmation that the group is cooperative rather than as a personal debt marker. They do not publicly thank specific rewarders or attempt to form explicit bilateral alliances based on reward exchange. When they do receive rewards, they accept them silently and maintain their high contribution level, suggesting they interpret rewards as encouragement to stay on the cooperative track rather than as a reason to change strategy. Overall, their reciprocity is generalized and group-focused rather than dyadic: they respond to a cooperative, rewarding environment by staying generous to the group, not by tracking and repaying individuals one-to-one.

They are rewarded in several rounds by others but show no explicit verbal acknowledgment or gratitude in chat. Any reciprocity they express must therefore be behavioral; however, without a mapping from their reward choices to specific targets, it is unknown whether they specifically reciprocate toward their own rewarders versus rewarding other cooperators. Their continued high contributions after being rewarded suggest that positive reinforcement does not change their strategy much; they are already at a cooperative ceiling. They also keep contributing fully and sometimes spend on rewards even in rounds where they receive nothing, which implies their motivation to reward is not contingent on being personally appreciated. Overall, observable reciprocity toward specific rewarders is unknown; at the broader level, they maintain a generous pattern regardless of personal treatment.

The player appears sensitive to being rewarded but does not alter their contribution pattern in response; they remain fully cooperative whether they are rewarded or ignored. Any effect of being rewarded manifests, if at all, in their use of the reward mechanism, not in their own contribution level. When they receive rewards from others, they do not clearly shift into a stable mutual-reward alliance with those specific individuals. After being rewarded, they sometimes increase or sustain substantial reward outflows in general, but they also at other times continue rewarding even when they are not rewarded back, and in some phases they reward heavily without receiving any reward. This makes direct reciprocity toward specific rewarders uncertain or at best weak: there is no clear one-to-one matching of “you rewarded me, so I now reward you.” Instead, their behavior fits an impersonal, norm-focused pattern: they appear to reward people based on current cooperative behavior rather than on whether those players previously rewarded them. They do not seem to retaliate against non-rewarders, nor to withdraw cooperation when rewards to them stop. Gratitude, if present, is not expressed as selective, partner-specific reciprocity but rather as maintaining or amplifying a general culture of rewarding cooperators. In terms of social style, they look like a principled cooperator who values stable, high-contribution norms over bilateral exchange relationships. They are willing to keep cooperating and often to keep rewarding even when that stance is not materially reciprocated, suggesting a personality that is persistent, norm-driven, and relatively insensitive to personal payoff inequalities, while still conditionally allocating rewards to reinforce cooperative behavior and withhold them from defectors.

This player receives reward multiple times from specific others, including when they have contributed both relatively high and relatively low amounts. They never reciprocate reward in kind to any partner, regardless of who rewarded them, the size of the reward, or the timing. They also do not increase their contribution in a systematic, sustained way immediately after being rewarded. Any upward shifts in contribution occur in various contexts and are not tightly aligned with receiving reward. This pattern suggests that they do not interpret reward as a relational signal that obliges reciprocity. They do not appear to engage in alliance-building via rewards, nor do they single out generous rewarders for extra cooperative treatment. Their response is closer to treating reward as a windfall: they accept the benefits without altering their own pro-social or strategic stance. There is no sign of gratitude signaling (no “payback” via higher contributions or outgoing rewards), nor of spiteful reactions when someone rewards others but not them. Their type is thus non-reciprocal with respect to the reward channel: they may respond somewhat to overall contribution trends, but not specifically to who has rewarded them.

This player continues to fully cooperate regardless of whether they receive little, moderate, or relatively higher amounts of reward, indicating that their underlying cooperative rule does not depend on being rewarded. They do not escalate their own reward spending in lockstep with changes in reward they receive, so direct reciprocal “you rewarded me, I’ll reward you back” behavior is not clearly present. Because the record does not reveal who rewarded them, it is unknown whether they attempt to reciprocate to specific individuals. Their contribution behavior is stable even when they receive no net advantage from others’ rewards, which suggests that they do not condition their cooperation on personal recognition or alliance benefits. They seem relatively low-reactive: being rewarded does not visibly change their main decision (full contribution), and any shifts in reward-giving are limited and not obviously synchronized with reward receipts. This fits a personality style that values cooperation for its own sake and treats rewards as secondary or experimental rather than as the core basis for alliances or tit-for-tat reciprocity. Whether they feel gratitude internally or attempt subtle alliance building through targeted reward is unknown, but their observable behavior suggests they remain principled and steady rather than strategically reciprocal.

The player appears responsive to being rewarded but not in a purely transactional or tit-for-tat manner. When they receive modest to moderate rewards, they do not reduce their own high contribution level or their generally high reward spending; instead, they continue to behave cooperatively and generous with rewards even when the net exchange is unfavorable to them. This indicates that they interpret receiving rewards as confirmation or support of their cooperative identity rather than as a strict contract requiring exact matching. They do not clearly condition their behavior on how much they personally are rewarded: there are episodes where their incoming rewards are relatively low yet their own reward spending and contributions remain high. Thus, they do not exhibit narrow direct reciprocity (e.g., “I only reward those who reward me”) as a dominant pattern. Any attempt at alliance building with specific rewarders is unknown from the data, because we cannot see whom they target. However, the stability of their generosity suggests a general openness to forming cooperative ties and reinforcing others, rather than a focus on small exclusive alliances. If they do reciprocate to specific rewarders, it is embedded in an overall strategy of broad prosocial reinforcement, not restricted to quid-pro-quo exchanges. Their type is best characterized as a robust, norm-driven cooperator who appreciates reward but does not make their core cooperative decisions hinge on how much they personally are recognized.

The player is notably responsive and relational when receiving rewards. They explicitly thank specific players for even small rewards and acknowledge them by name, reinforcing social bonds. They reciprocate within a defined alliance, willing to send equal or greater rewards to those who reward them and to those included in the “team.” They also use chat to clarify who is actually rewarding whom, monitoring consistency and using that information to adjust trust. When a player’s reward to them is missing or smaller than expected, they do not retaliate materially but update their expressed trust and future inclusion in reward arrangements. They express appreciation to consistent rewarders, accept apologies or explanations for missed rewards, and remain willing to cooperate with them. Overall, they treat rewards as an ongoing relationship currency—thanking, reciprocating, and adjusting alliances based on observed rewarding behavior, without abandoning their overarching cooperative stance.

They appear to receive no rewards from others (no reward income is recorded for them), so there is no observed behavior in response to being rewarded. They do not thank anyone for rewards, do not talk about who rewarded whom, and do not reference any personal reward experience. Consequently, their reciprocity tendencies toward identifiable rewarders remain unknown. Given their stable full contributions but zero reward spending, it is plausible they rely on unconditional public-good cooperation rather than targeted reciprocity, but this is an inference with low confidence since there is no direct stimulus-response episode to evaluate.

They repeatedly receive sizable rewards from other players but show minimal behavioral reciprocity. They signal interest in being rewarded (directly asking for it and affirming when others propose sending to them) and appear to track when rewards are missed, but when it is their turn to reciprocate, they mostly do not act. When someone calls them out for not rewarding in line with a tacit agreement, they briefly acknowledge in chat and make a verbal commitment to “get them next time,” which again is not reliably honored. When another player publicly labels them a cheat, they answer with a short, mild objection rather than with compensating behavior. Overall, they appreciate receiving rewards and want to remain eligible for them, yet they avoid the costly reciprocation that would consolidate those alliances, relying instead on words and the inertia of others’ generosity.

The player does not show strong or reliable reciprocity toward those who reward them. Although rewarders are identifiable in the environment, there is no consistent shift toward higher contributions or more generous rewarding that clearly follows receipt of rewards. Any incidental correlation between being rewarded and temporarily contributing more is not stable enough to establish a deliberate reciprocation strategy. They seem to treat incoming rewards primarily as windfall income rather than as a normative signal that demands a response. Their continued tendency to free-ride even while being rewarded by others indicates that they do not strongly internalize others’ positive signals as obligations to cooperate. Alliance-building through reward reciprocity is absent or very weak. There is no visible formation of stable “you reward me, I reward you, we both cooperate” dynamics. Instead, they remain largely focused on maximizing their own payoffs, sometimes accepting benefits from cooperators and rewarders without adjusting behavior in a way that would clearly sustain those relationships. Emotionally or normatively, we can infer only that they are not strongly driven by gratitude or guilt in this setting. They accept help and high contributions from others, plus occasional rewards, yet keep their own costly prosocial actions sparse and inconsistent. Whether this reflects a coldly strategic mindset, low concern for social norms, or uncertainty about what others expect is unknown, but in operational terms, they are not reliably reciprocating or alliance-seeking when rewarded.

When this player is rewarded, they show some sensitivity but not simple, mechanical reciprocity. They do not automatically respond by reducing their own contributions or by stopping rewards; nor do they mechanically “pay back” specific rewarders in a one-for-one manner that is clearly observable from the aggregate record alone (who exactly receives their rewards is unknown). Therefore, specific reciprocation patterns toward particular identities are unknown. What is observable is that receiving rewards coincides with them maintaining or resuming high contributions in several episodes, even when average group cooperation is uneven. This suggests that being rewarded reinforces their willingness to continue playing as a high or very high contributor, at least for some time. They appear to interpret reward as confirmation that their cooperative behavior is noticed and valued, which sustains their prosocial stance. At the same time, they do not become unconditionally cooperative just because they are rewarded. After having been rewarded in some periods, they still later revert to low contributions when group behavior looks weak or unstable. This indicates that while they are responsive to positive social feedback, they remain strategically conditional and attentive to the broader cooperation level, not just to the behavior of their rewarders. In interpersonal style terms, they function as a “reciprocity-aware conditional cooperator”: reward from others encourages them and makes high contribution more likely, but they do not lock into blind loyalty. Their alliances, if any, are based on ongoing patterns of contribution and mutual reinforcement rather than on a simple tit-for-tat repayment rule. Overall, they are positively responsive to being rewarded but keep a strategic, group-level perspective rather than purely dyadic reciprocity.

The player receives rewards from others in several rounds, but their own strategy does not visibly hinge on whether they are being rewarded at that moment. They neither escalate contributions after being rewarded (they are already at or near maximum) nor withdraw cooperation when rewards to them decrease or disappear. This indicates relatively weak behavioral dependence on personal reinforcement and a stronger adherence to internally chosen norms. There is no clear sign of targeted reciprocal rewarding (e.g., selectively rewarding those who reward them); instead, their rewarding appears more aligned with peers’ contribution levels than with who previously favored them. Thus, they do not strongly pursue bilateral alliances or mutual back-scratching; their orientation is more group-norm-focused than dyadic. Their reaction to being rewarded is best characterized as appreciative but not strategically responsive: they continue cooperating and sometimes rewarding regardless of their own receipt of rewards, showing low emphasis on direct reciprocity and higher emphasis on upholding a general cooperative standard.

When they receive reward, they do not visibly change their contribution level (which is already maximal at those times), so any reciprocal response cannot show up in contributions. In reward behavior, there are indications of conditional responsiveness but not tight one‑to‑one reciprocity. After being rewarded, they sometimes become more active as a rewarder in subsequent play, but they do not simply mirror specific individuals; they appear to direct rewards toward high cooperators in general rather than exclusively toward those who rewarded them. This suggests they treat incoming rewards more as validation of a shared cooperative norm than as a transaction that must be individually repaid. They therefore lean toward generalized reciprocity or norm‑based reciprocity (“support the cooperative group, not just my benefactor”) rather than strict dyadic tit‑for‑tat. Whether they keep track of exactly who rewarded them and deliberately favor those players later is uncertain; the data do not allow clear identification of targeted reciprocal patterns. Their overall style toward rewarders is best summarized as appreciative but group‑oriented: they continue to support cooperators broadly rather than forming narrow reward cliques.

They receive rewards on multiple occasions but never reciprocate with rewards to others. Since rewarders are identifiable to the rewarded, their lack of response is informative: they do not engage in direct reciprocity toward those who acknowledge their contributions. This points to a low propensity for gratitude-based costly reciprocation, at least via the formal reward mechanism. At the same time, their contribution behavior does not show a strong shift upward after being rewarded; any increases or decreases in their contributions appear consistent with their broader pattern rather than targeted responses to specific reward events. This implies that they treat rewards more as windfall gains than as social signals that call for adjustment or alliance-building. Thus, their persona in response to reward is: appreciative only in a purely payoff sense, not behaviorally reciprocal. They accept extra income but do not alter contribution levels to favor rewarders or to reinforce cooperative ties, and they do not join in the “norm of rewarding cooperators” even after being a beneficiary of that norm.

They receive reward at least once and do not visibly change behavior in response; they remain a full contributor before and after. They do not verbally acknowledge being rewarded, nor do they explicitly reciprocate to identifiable rewarders in chat, so their emotional or social reaction is unknown. There is no clear behavioral shift in their use of reward following being rewarded, making it difficult to infer direct reciprocity toward specific partners. If they ever reward those who previously rewarded them, that cannot be confirmed from the available information (targets are not shown). Overall, they behave as a stable cooperator whose actions are not contingent on whether they personally receive rewards, and any alliance-building or tit-for-tat reciprocity toward specific rewarders is unknown or at best low-confidence.

They consistently receive moderate amounts of reward from others but never reciprocate in a targeted fashion. Their reward spending does not map onto those who previously rewarded them; indeed, for most of the game they choose not to reward anyone at all despite having multiple identifiable rewarders. This implies that they do not practice direct reciprocity toward benefactors and do not try to form explicit bilateral “reward alliances.” The lack of reciprocal reward suggests either that they place little value on using the reward channel for relationship-building, or that they view direct reciprocity as too costly given the reward technology. They appear to accept being rewarded without feeling obligation to “pay it back.” At the same time, they do not retaliate or reduce contributions when rewarders stop or when they themselves are not rewarded; their cooperative behavior is decoupled from whether others reward them. This points to a relatively unconditional cooperative identity rather than a tit-for-tat or gratitude-based strategy. Overall, in environments where rewarders are identifiable, they behaves as a “non-reciprocating cooperator”: appreciative enough to keep contributing, but unwilling to spend in-kind rewards to acknowledge or reinforce those who helped them.

This player receives small rewards on several occasions from others but never reciprocates with reward spending. They do not respond in kind to individuals who reward them or to the broader pattern of others paying costs to encourage cooperation. Their contribution behavior does not clearly shift toward greater cooperation in direct response to being rewarded; they continue to alternate between contributing and free-riding based on their own apparent payoff calculus rather than out of loyalty or gratitude. There is no evidence that they treat identifiable rewarders differently from non-rewarders in either contribution or reward decisions. This indicates low reciprocity in terms of costly actions: they accept benefits but do not incur costs to return favors, and they do not appear to engage in alliance-building or tit-for-tat reward exchanges. Their attitude toward being rewarded is best described as passive acceptance without behavioral obligation.

Because identities of rewarders are visible, this player can track who supports them. Across the game, they do sometimes receive rewards, but they do not restrict their own rewards only to those who have helped them. This implies that they do not define alliances narrowly as “mutual reward clubs.” Instead, they treat rewarders as part of a broader set of potentially cooperative partners, but do not exhibit a strong, exclusive reciprocity pattern in return giving based solely on who has rewarded them. They also keep rewarding even when their own rewards are relatively small or absent. This indicates that their willingness to reward is not highly conditional on being rewarded first; if anything, their reward behavior is weakly sensitive to direct reciprocity and more rooted in their assessment of others’ contributions and the cooperative structure of the group. There is no clear evidence of punishment-by-withholding-reward directed specifically at previous rewarders who change behavior; their reward pattern does not sharply flip or shut off in response to single feedback events. As a result, their interpersonal stance toward rewarders can best be described as “open but not strongly reciprocal”: they appreciate and accept rewards but do not anchor their entire strategy on tit‑for‑tat reward exchange.

The player is never rewarded by anyone and also never rewards others, so there is no direct evidence of how they respond to identifiable rewarders. When others reward them, they do not respond by rewarding back, even when they know who has given them positive payoff transfers. They may feel gratitude internally, but behaviorally they do not express it through the available costly action; whether this reflects an intentional stance (“I cooperate, but I don’t pay for extra transfers”) or simple inertia/indifference to the reward mechanism is unknown.

This player is never the recipient of rewards, so there is no direct evidence of reciprocity toward benefactors. This player receives reward from others but does not mirror it back to those specific individuals in a direct tit-for-tat fashion. This implies a preference for generalized cooperation rather than for relationship-specific exchange. Whether they consciously note who rewarded them is unknown, but their behavior indicates that any such awareness does not dominate their reward choices.

They receive rewards from multiple others across the game, sometimes substantial, but they do not clearly reciprocate in a targeted way toward specific rewarders. They receive rewards in multiple periods from different players. They also do not appear to “join” the extreme, mutually-rewarding cliques that burn huge amounts of coins on each other, suggesting they are not easily drawn into coalitionary reward spirals.

The player sometimes receives rewards from others, but there is no clear pattern of direct reciprocity toward specific rewarders. They are repeatedly rewarded by various players and sometimes receive quite large net transfers, yet they never reciprocate with rewards or punishment toward their benefactors. When rewarded, the player does not show strong, stable reciprocity specifically tied to those rewards. This player never receives any rewards, so their direct response to being rewarded is unknown. When they receive a reward, they do not change their subsequent contribution level or begin to use the reward or punishment systems. They are rewarded at least twice and can clearly see that someone chose to benefit them, yet they offer no return reward. Thus, their response to being rewarded is passive: they accept the benefit, maintain or resume cooperative contributions, but do not invest in reinforcing or reciprocating toward the rewarder specifically. When rewarded, they do not reciprocate in any observable way. Their contributions stay at zero and they do not respond by rewarding others in turn. This player is rewarded by others but shows no observable change in their own sanctioning patterns toward those rewarders or the group afterward, beyond what is already described in their contribution and reward behaviors. They are rewarded several times, often by highly cooperative peers, but they never adjust their already high contributions in response; contributions are already at the ceiling. This suggests that gratitude-driven direct reciprocity is not their primary motive; they are focused on contributions and norm adherence, not on personal favor exchange. Being rewarded does not change their pattern of norm enforcement or target selection. Their response to being rewarded is functionally “neutral plus consistent”: they continue doing what they were already doing—maximizing contributions, rewarding cooperators, and punishing defectors—without visible strategic adjustment tied to who has rewarded them. Overall, this player’s type is a principled, strong norm-enforcing altruistic cooperator. They maintain maximum cooperation regardless of exploitation risk, invest heavily in punishing under-contributors and especially repeat offenders, and generously reward high contributors. They appear driven by fairness norms and group-level cooperation rather than short-term payoff maximization or personalized reciprocity, and they remain committed to these norms through to the end of the game.

They are rewarded once near the end by another player but do not reciprocate, either immediately or later, and offer no verbal acknowledgment. This suggests that while they do not reject positive incentives, they also do not treat them as a basis for special reciprocity or coalition formation.

They do receive rewards from others in a few rounds but do not comment on them in chat, do not reciprocate with reward spending, and do not shift their behavior in any observable way. When others call out their generosity, they respond kindly and modestly, explicitly acknowledging the thanks. This shows they attend to social recognition and are comfortable in a prosocial identity. If any reciprocity exists, it is diffuse rather than targeted: they maintain a broad rewarding pattern rather than forming tight bilateral exchange. Overall, they exhibit soft reciprocity in tone (saying thanks, staying generous) rather than in narrow, calculated tit-for-tat.

The player receives rewards from several others over time, including from players who are also strong cooperators and sometimes strong punishers. This indicates a form of generalized, rather than strictly dyadic, reciprocity: being rewarded reinforces their sense of who belongs to the “cooperative in-group,” and they are more likely to treat that group generously (via contributions and occasional rewards), but not necessarily on a one-to-one basis. They do not appear to punish those who previously rewarded them, even when those rewarders later engage in controversial punishment; this suggests some loyalty or at least a reluctance to directly attack past benefactors. However, the mapping from “was rewarded by X” to “later rewards X” is inconsistent, so explicit quid-pro-quo reciprocity is weak; gratitude is expressed more through continued cooperation with the group than through precise individual payback.

When others reward them, they do not adjust their core behaviors in any obvious way. They receive rewards from some group members but never explicitly acknowledge or reciprocate them in chat. They receive positive rewards in some rounds but do not alter their behavior in any noticeable way as a response. The player is occasionally rewarded by others but shows no clear pattern of targeted reciprocity: they do not systematically reward those who reward them, nor do they form obvious bilateral “alliances” through mutual reward. Instead, their reward choices remain focused on a subset of others they appear to like or approve of for reasons that include but are not limited to cooperation level. When rewarded, they do not dramatically shift their contribution pattern; they continue with a mixed strategy of defection and full contribution. Their default posture toward rewarders appears appreciative rather than strategic, but precise beliefs (e.g., gratitude, desire to reciprocate in future games) are unknown.

They themselves are rewarded a small number of times but never respond in any observable, contingent way. Their own contribution behavior is already maximally cooperative and does not change after being rewarded, and their pattern of whom they reward does not shift in any clear reciprocity toward those who reward them.
